title: Execution Contexts
concept: Running tool handlers in isolated environments (like Docker or temporary virtualenvs) for security.

exercises.prompt: Quick warm-up! In an AI agent, what is a 'tool'?

options.text: A ready-made action the agent can call, like searching the web or running code
A physical hammer the robot holds
The name of the AI company

exercises.explain: A tool is an action the agent can trigger, like 'search the web', 'send an email', or 'run this code'. This lesson looks at WHERE that code should run safely.

cards.title: Would you let a stranger run code on your laptop?
cards.body: When an AI agent uses a tool, it often runs real code. If that code runs directly on your computer, a mistake or attack could delete files or steal data.

cards.title: What is an execution context?
cards.body: An execution context is the isolated 'box' where a tool's code actually runs. Using tools like Docker or a temporary virtualenv keeps that code walled off from your real system.

cards.title: Like a science lab glovebox
cards.body: Scientists handle dangerous chemicals inside a sealed glovebox, not on the open kitchen table. An isolated context lets code do its work without touching everything else.

cards.title: A real example
cards.body: An agent needs to run some Python a user pasted in. Instead of running it on the server, it spins up a fresh Docker container, runs the code there, grabs the result, then throws the container away.

cards.title: Myth: 'Sandboxing just slows things down'
cards.body: It does add a little overhead, but that's the point of safety. One bad tool call without isolation can wreck a whole system, which is far more costly than a few seconds of setup.

cards.title: Direct vs isolated
cards.body: Running tool code on your real machine risks everything. Running it in a disposable box limits the damage.

left.text: Code touches your real files, network, and secrets. One bug can be a disaster.

right.text: Code runs in a sealed box that gets deleted afterward. Damage stays trapped inside.

exercises.prompt: Why do agents often run tool code inside Docker or a temporary virtualenv?

options.text: To keep risky code isolated from the real system for security
To make the code run in a different language
To make the AI model bigger
To hide the code from the user permanently

exercises.explain: The main reason is security and isolation: if the code misbehaves, the damage is trapped inside the disposable box instead of harming your real machine.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Running untrusted tool code directly on your main server is riskier than running it in an isolated container.
exercises.explain: True. Direct execution exposes your files, secrets, and network. An isolated container walls the code off, so a mistake or attack stays contained.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about safe tool execution.
exercises.sentence: An isolated execution context keeps risky tool code walled off from your real system.
exercises.explain: An 'isolated' context is the key idea: the code runs in its own sealed box, separated from everything you care about.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of execution contexts sounds AI-generated (smooth but generic)?

options.text: An execution context is a fundamental construct that provides a robust, secure, and scalable environment for the seamless execution of computational tasks.
Just run the code in a throwaway Docker box so if it breaks, only the box breaks.
Think glovebox: the code does its thing inside, and your real machine stays clean.

exercises.explain: The first is packed with vague buzzwords like 'robust, secure, and scalable' and 'seamless' without any concrete picture. The others use plain, specific images.

exercises.prompt: An agent gets a tool call to run Python code submitted by an unknown user. What is the safest design?

options.text: Spin up a fresh, isolated container with limited access, run the code there, capture the result, then destroy the container
Run the code directly on the main server so it's faster
Run the code with full admin rights so it never fails
Skip running it and just guess the output

exercises.explain: Untrusted code should always run in a disposable, isolated context with limited permissions. That way, if it's buggy or malicious, the harm is trapped in the box and thrown away with it. Speed and admin power come at the cost of safety, and guessing defeats the tool's purpose.
